content.text: What a North-Star KPI Is

A north-star KPI is the single metric that best represents whether a GenAI feature is delivering its intended business value. It is not a technical metric, not an average of several metrics, and not the metric the engineering team finds easiest to improve. It is the number that a business sponsor would use to answer the question: 'Is this product worth continuing to invest in?'

North-star thinking forces a choice. Most early-stage GenAI programmes measure everything and prioritise nothing, producing dashboards that are rich in data and silent on value. The north-star discipline requires agreeing — before launch — on the one number that the entire programme exists to move.

attrs.alt: North-star KPI hierarchy: north star → driver KPIs → output metrics

content.text: Anatomy of a North-Star KPI
A well-formed north-star KPI has six properties that distinguish it from an output metric or a vanity number:
Property
Requirement
FNOL chatbot example
Outcome-linked
Measures a change in business or user behaviour, not AI system behaviour
Cost per FNOL claim resolved — a finance line item
Single number
Aggregates the programme's value into one formula
£ per clean claim (cost + quality in one expression)
Sponsor-legible
A non-technical stakeholder can explain it without coaching
'This is what it costs us to handle one claim correctly'
Measurable from existing data
Formula inputs exist in current systems or can be built in < 2 weeks
CRM + cloud billing + payroll (all available)
Sensitive to AI quality
Worsening AI quality makes the number worse, not just different
Rework claims increase denominator denominator, raising cost per clean claim
Time-horizoned
Has a target value and a date by which it should be reached
£38.60 within 12 months of launch
Dataclasses: North-Star KPI Framework

fnol = NorthStarFramework(
    feature_name="FNOL Claims Chatbot",

    north_star=KPI(
        name="cost_per_clean_claim",
        description="Fully-loaded cost per FNOL claim resolved without human rework within 48 h",
        formula="(AI_infra_cost + remaining_human_cost) / (total_claims - rework_claims)",
        unit="GBP",
        direction=Direction.LOWER,
        baseline=47.20,
        target=38.60,
        target_horizon="12 months",
        measurement_source="Finance ledger + CRM rework flag",
        cadence="Monthly",
        owner="Programme Sponsor / Finance",
    ),

    drivers=[
        KPI(
            name="automation_rate",
            description="Fraction of FNOL claims fully resolved by AI without human touch",
            formula="ai_resolved_claims / total_claims",
            unit="fraction",
            direction=Direction.HIGHER,
            baseline=0.0,
            target=0.65,
            target_horizon="12 months",
            measurement_source="CRM: claims with no human activity flag",
            cadence="Weekly",
            owner="Claims Operations",
            parent_kpi="cost_per_clean_claim",
        ),
        KPI(
            name="rework_rate",
            description="Fraction of AI-handled claims requiring human correction within 48 h",
            formula="rework_events / ai_handled_claims",
            unit="fraction",
            direction=Direction.LOWER,
            baseline=0.22,
            target=0.08,
            target_horizon="12 months",
            measurement_source="CRM: 'AI outcome amended' flag",
            cadence="Weekly",
            owner="Claims Quality",
            parent_kpi="cost_per_clean_claim",
        ),
        KPI(
            name="ai_cost_per_interaction",
            description="Fully-loaded AI cost per chatbot interaction (tokens + infra + amortised dev)",
            formula="(token_cost + infra_cost + dev_amortisation) / sessions",
            unit="GBP",
            direction=Direction.LOWER,
            baseline=0.0,    # no baseline — new capability
            target=0.80,     # P95 ceiling
            target_horizon="6 months",
            measurement_source="Token ledger + cloud billing + FinOps allocation",
            cadence="Weekly",
            owner="FinOps",
            parent_kpi="cost_per_clean_claim",
        ),
    ],

    output_metrics=[
        KPI(
            name="intent_accuracy",
            description="Fraction of user messages correctly classified by intent",
            formula="correct_intents / total_messages",
            unit="fraction",
            direction=Direction.HIGHER,
            baseline=0.0,
            target=0.94,
            target_horizon="launch",
            measurement_source="Weekly QA sample (n=200)",
            cadence="Weekly CI eval",
            owner="AI Quality",
            parent_kpi="automation_rate",
        ),
        KPI(
            name="hallucination_rate",
            description="Fraction of factual policy claims not supported by cited source",
            formula="unsupported_claims / total_claims_made",
            unit="fraction",
            direction=Direction.LOWER,
            baseline=0.0,
            target=0.02,
            target_horizon="launch",
            measurement_source="LLM-judge on weekly 100-case sample",
            cadence="Weekly CI eval",
            owner="AI Quality",
            parent_kpi="rework_rate",
        ),
    ],
)

print(fnol.summary())
# FNOL Claims Chatbot: NS=cost_per_clean_claim (3 drivers, 2 output metrics)

errors = fnol.validate()
print(errors)  # []

print(fnol.north_star.pct_improvement())   # -18.22 (18% reduction target)
Designing the North-Star Formula

The formula must be computable from systems that exist or can be built. Run a dry-run calculation before committing to the formula: if any input requires a new data pipeline that will take months to build, the north-star cannot be measured at launch and the programme will fly blind for months.

# North-star KPI formula validation: make the formula computable from day 1

import datetime

def compute_cost_per_clean_claim(
    period_start: datetime.date,
    period_end: datetime.date,
    ai_infra_cost: float,           # from cloud billing
    human_cost_for_ai_claims: float,# agent time on AI-routed claims
    total_fnol_claims: int,
    rework_claims: int,
) -> float:
    """
    Computes the north-star KPI for the given period.

    Note: human_cost_for_ai_claims captures residual human handling of claims
    that were initially routed to the AI but required human correction.
    It is NOT the total human cost of the claims function.
    """
    clean_claims = total_fnol_claims - rework_claims
    if clean_claims <= 0:
        raise ValueError("No clean claims in period — KPI undefined")

    total_cost = ai_infra_cost + human_cost_for_ai_claims
    return total_cost / clean_claims

# Example: Month 3 actuals
kpi_m3 = compute_cost_per_clean_claim(
    period_start=datetime.date(2024, 9, 1),
    period_end=datetime.date(2024, 9, 30),
    ai_infra_cost=12_400.00,        # £ cloud + inference
    human_cost_for_ai_claims=8_200.00,  # residual human on rework claims
    total_fnol_claims=5_200,
    rework_claims=720,              # 13.8% rework (tracking down from 22%)
)

print(f"Month 3 north-star KPI: £{kpi_m3:.2f}")
# Month 3 north-star KPI: £4.60 per clean claim
# Note: absolute cost is lower than baseline because AI handles most claims at much lower unit cost
# The relevant comparison is vs the baseline per-claim cost trajectory

Common formula design mistakes: including cost categories that are allocated by accounting convention rather than causally driven by AI activity (making the north-star insensitive to AI improvements), or using average cost when the distribution is right-skewed (outlier claims distort the mean while the median is actually improving).
Driver KPIs: Decomposing the North Star

A north-star KPI is typically composed of 2–4 driver KPIs — outcome-level metrics that each explain a portion of the north star's variance. Driver KPIs are the operational levers: the programme can diagnose which driver is underperforming and direct engineering effort toward it.
Driver KPI
Relationship to north star
When it moves the north star
Automation rate (↑)
Higher automation → fewer human-hours per claim → lower cost
When AI can handle ≥ 65% of claims without human touch
Rework rate (↓)
Lower rework → fewer claims in denominator loss → cost per clean claim falls
When hallucination rate and intent accuracy both improve
AI cost per interaction (↓)
Lower token cost → lower AI_infra_cost in numerator
When prompt compression and caching reduce average context length

Driver KPIs should be set up in an influence diagram before finalising the north star formula: draw the causal arrows from each driver to the north star and verify that moving a driver in the right direction always improves the north star. If a driver can improve while the north star worsens (because of an unmeasured confound), it is not a valid driver.
The Tracking Plan

A north-star KPI without a tracking plan is a declaration of intent. The tracking plan specifies every data input to the formula, its source system, its owner, its measurement lag, and the reporting cadence. Agreeing this before launch avoids the common post-launch situation where the KPI cannot be computed because a required data source is not instrumented.

The most common tracking plan failure is measurement lag disagreement: the cloud bill arrives on a different cadence than the CRM data, making monthly KPI computation impossible without a data engineering pipeline that nobody built. Identify and resolve all lag mismatches before launch.
Setting the Target

The north-star KPI target must be derived from a business case calculation, not chosen as a round number. A target that cannot be back-calculated from a business case is not defensible when it is missed — and a target that is trivially achievable generates no programme momentum.
Start with the investment: total programme cost (dev, infra, change management) amortised over 3 years.
Calculate the implied saving required to justify the investment at the expected return threshold (e.g. 2× payback within 3 years).
Convert the required saving into a cost-per-claim reduction using the projected claim volume.
Verify the target is achievable: check that the required automation rate and rework rate are consistent with what comparable AI deployments have achieved.
Add a confidence margin: if the programme needs a 20% cost reduction to justify investment, set the 12-month milestone target at 18% (allowing headroom for ramp-up).
Input
Value
Source
Total programme cost (3-year)
Finance: dev + infra + change management
Required payback multiple
Finance policy
Required 3-year saving
Annual claim volume (projected)
120,000 claims
Operations forecast
Required cost reduction per claim
£4.2m ÷ 360k claims
Current cost per clean claim
Finance baseline
Required target cost per clean claim
12-month milestone (with 8% confidence margin)
£35.53 × 1.085 (partial ramp-up)
Monthly Review Format

The north-star KPI review is the primary cadence for programme governance. It is not a technical meeting — output metrics are summarised only when a driver is off-track. The structure below keeps the review focussed on the business question: are we on track to hit the target?

# Monthly north-star KPI review: structured report template

MONTHLY_KPI_REPORT = """
FNOL AI Programme — North Star KPI Review
Period: {month} {year}
Prepared: {prep_date}

1. NORTH STAR KPI
   ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   cost_per_clean_claim:  £{actual:.2f}  (target: £{target:.2f}, baseline: £47.20)
   Progress to target:    {pct_progress:.0f}% of £{improvement_needed:.2f} gap closed
   Status:                {status}   [GREEN / AMBER / RED]

2. DRIVER KPIs
   ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   automation_rate:       {auto_rate:.1%}  (target: 65.0%)
   rework_rate:           {rework_rate:.1%}  (target: 8.0%)
   ai_cost_per_session:   £{session_cost:.4f}  (target: ≤ £0.80 P95)

3. ROOT CAUSE (if any driver is AMBER or RED)
   ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   {root_cause}

4. NEXT 30-DAY ACTIONS
   ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   {actions}

5. RISK TO ANNUAL TARGET
   ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   {risk_statement}
"""

Status classification: GREEN if actual is within 5% of the prorated target trajectory; AMBER if 5–15% behind trajectory; RED if more than 15% behind or if trend is worsening over three consecutive months. RED requires an immediate root cause report and a recovery plan within 10 working days.
When to Have Multiple North-Star KPIs

A GenAI feature should have exactly one north-star KPI per business objective. If a feature serves multiple distinct objectives (e.g. cost reduction AND customer satisfaction improvement), it may legitimately have two north-star KPIs — but each must be owned by a different stakeholder and tracked separately. Combining them into a composite index loses the diagnostic signal.
Scenario
North-star KPIs
Why not one?
FNOL chatbot: cost + quality
Cost per clean claim AND claimant CSAT
Cost and satisfaction can move in opposite directions — combining them hides the trade-off
Legal doc summariser: time + accuracy
Hours saved per lawyer-week AND rework rate on summaries
A faster but more inaccurate system improves one and worsens the other
Customer service bot: deflection + NPS
Deflection rate AND post-interaction NPS
High deflection with low NPS means the bot is blocking, not helping

When two north-star KPIs are defined, the programme must specify a priority ordering: if they are in tension, which one takes precedence? This decision belongs to the business sponsor, not the product team.
Common North-Star KPI Design Mistakes
Mistake
Consequence
Correct practice
Choosing an output metric as north star
Engineering optimises the metric without improving business value
North star must be an outcome metric measuring business or user behaviour change
Formula inputs not available at launch
KPI cannot be computed; programme flies blind for months
Run dry-run calculation from existing systems before committing to formula
Target not tied to business case
Target is arbitrary; missed without consequence; hit without generating the expected return
Derive target from investment payback calculation
More than one north star per objective
Mixed signals; programme cannot prioritise
One north star per business objective; if multiple objectives, two north stars with explicit priority ordering
North star reviewed only at programme completion
Problems not visible until too late to correct
Monthly review cadence from launch month 1
Driver KPIs not linked to north star by causal model
Drivers can improve while north star worsens
Draw influence diagram; verify each driver's direction of effect
Summary

A north-star KPI is the single metric that answers whether a GenAI feature is worth its investment. It must be outcome-linked, single-valued, sponsor-legible, measurable from existing data, sensitive to AI quality, and time-horizoned with a target derived from the business case. Driver KPIs decompose the north star into operational levers, and output metrics explain driver variance. The tracking plan specifies every data input, its source, lag, and owner before launch. Monthly reviews compare actual against a prorated trajectory and classify status as GREEN, AMBER, or RED — triggering a recovery plan when RED. A feature with no north-star KPI has no agreed definition of success and no way to know whether it is delivering value.
